statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You are given an integer array $$$a\_1, a\_2, a\_3, \\dots, a\_n$$$. Your task is to make all elements of $$$a$$$ equal. In order to do it, you can perform the following operation at most $$$k$$$ times:

- choose any index $$$i$$$ from $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$ and increase $$$a\_i$$$ by one.

You can choose any element of the array, but if the chosen $$$a\_i$$$ is strictly greater than the current minimum of the array, you'll earn one coin.What is the maximum number of coins you can earn among all possible ways to make the array equal?

Input Format
The first line contains one integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \\le t \\le 1000$$$) â€” the number of test cases. Next, $$$t$$$ cases follow (all cases are independent).

The first line of each test case contains two integers $$$n$$$ and $$$k$$$ ($$$2 \\le n \\le 3 \\cdot 10^5$$$; $$$1 \\le k \\le 10^{12}$$$) â€” the size of array $$$a$$$ and the maximum number of operations you can perform.

The second line contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$a\_1, a\_2, \\dots, a\_n$$$ ($$$1 \\le a\_i \\le 10^9$$$) â€” the array itself.

It's guaranteed that the total sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases doesn't exceed $$$3 \\cdot 10^5$$$.

Output Format
For each test case, if it's impossible to make all elements equal, print $$$-1$$$. Otherwise, print the maximum number of coins you can earn while making all elements equal.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
3 16
1 10 2
4 20
6 2 4 9
5 9
7 7 7 7 7
2 1000000000000
1000000000 1000000000
Output
-1
11
0
499999999999

Note
In the first test case, you need at least $$$17$$$ operations to make all elements equal (or to get the array $$$\[10, 10, 10\]$$$).

In the second test case, you can, for example, increase $$$a\_3 = 4$$$ to $$$10$$$, then increase $$$a\_1 = 6$$$ to $$$10$$$, after that increase $$$a\_4 = 9$$$ to $$$10$$$ and, finally, $$$a\_2 = 2$$$ to $$$10$$$. You'll perform $$$19$$$ operations and earn $$$(10 - 4) + (10 - 6) + (10 - 9) = 11$$$ coins in total, since increasing the $$$a\_2$$$ from $$$2$$$ to $$$10$$$ won't give you any coins.

In the third test case, you can leave the array unchanged or make all elements equal to $$$8$$$ â€” both strategies will give you $$$0$$$ coins.

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2141D - Avoid Minimums
Let's say that an operation that gives you a coin is a "good" operation.
Suppose that the resulting value of elements of the array $$$a$$$ is $$$v$$$. Let's prove that it's optimal to make $$$v$$$ as big as possible. It will be obvious if we prove that the total profit to make $$$v$$$ is greater than or equal to the total profit of making $$$v - 1$$$.
Let's take the best strategy to make values $$$v - 1$$$ and then let's increase each $$$a_i$$$ by $$$1$$$. We'll get the whole array equal to $$$v$$$. Each new operation will be "bad" (since each element is equal to the current minimum), so the
current
profit will be equal to the maximum profit to make $$$v - 1$$$. But it means that the
maximum
profit for $$$v$$$ is at least the maximum profit for $$$v - 1$$$ or even bigger. So, maximizing the resulting value $$$v$$$ is always optimal.
Let's calculate the maximum value $$$v$$$: since we have only $$$k$$$ operations, then the total increase of elements can't exceed $$$k$$$. In other words, we need the maximum $$$v$$$ such that $$$n \cdot v - \sum_{i=1}^{n}{a_i} \le k$$$, or $$$v = \left\lfloor \frac{k + \sum_{i=1}^{n}{a_i}}{n} \right\rfloor$$$.
Ok, we found the desired value $$$v$$$, now we need to maximize the number of good operations. Since the total number of operations to reach $$$v$$$ is constant now, we can minimize the number of bad operations instead.
What is the minimum possible number of bad operations? Obviously, each $$$a_i$$$ that is equal to the minimum in the initial array needs at least one bad operation to increase it.
Also, since the array $$$a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$$$ transforms to $$$v, v, \dots, v$$$, then its minimum will go from $$$\min{a_i}$$$ to $$$v$$$, "visiting" all intermediate values $$$(\min{a_i} + 1), (\min{a_i} + 2), \dots, (v - 1)$$$. Since for each intermediate value there will be at least one minimum equal to it, then there will be at least one bad operation for each intermediate value.
In total, the number of bad operations can't be less than $$$(\text{number of minimums in $$$a$$$}) + (v - \min{a_i} - 1)$$$. And, actually, it can be reached with the following greedy algorithm: let's iterate $$$a_i$$$ in descending order and increase them to $$$v$$$ one by one.
The number of good operations is equal to the total number of operations $$$n \cdot v - \sum{a_i}$$$ minus the number of bad operations, and can be calculated in $$$O(n)$$$ time. Note that the formula for bad operations assumes that $$$v  \gt  \min{a_i}$$$.

solutions.title: Solution (adedalic)
solutions.content:
fun main
()
{
repeat
(
readln
().
toInt
())
{
val
(
n
,
k
)
=
readln
().
split
(
' '
).
map
{
it
.
toLong
()
}
val a
=
readln
().
split
(
' '
).
map
{
it
.
toLong
()
}
// n * resVal - a.sum() <= k
val resVal
=
(
k
+
a
.
sum
())
/
n
if
(
resVal
<
a
.
maxOrNull
()!!)
{
println
(-
1
)
return
@repeat
}
val minEl
=
a
.
minOrNull
()!!
var
ans
=
a
.
sumOf
{
resVal
-
it
}
-
(
resVal
-
minEl
)
if
(
minEl
<
resVal
)
ans
-=
a
.
count
{
it
==
minEl
}
-
1
println
(
ans
)
}
}
